speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want to set that stand there, I'll be able to-- --this and put it in? Yeah, you can just take-- just editor. Maybe I'll just take your assistance by itself. Or a little take. Go ahead. How's that? Good. OK. This is the slate for June 21, 1995. Abraham Melezin is the survivor. He is being interviewed by Miriam Horowitz. The city is Teaneck, New Jersey, USA. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And there we are. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tell me I can start? My name is Miriam Horowitz-- M-I-R-I-A-M H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z. This is June 21, 1995. I'm interviewing Abraham Melezin in Teaneck, New Jersey, in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. And we're-- we're underway here. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you give us your name, please, and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Abraham Melezin, A-B-R-A-H-A-M M-E-L-E-Z-I-N, N like 'Nancy.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other names that you have used? And could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: During, during the war, when I left the ghetto and I went on so-called Aryan side, I used the name Adam Mielzynski, A-D-A-M M-I-E-L-Z-Y-N-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us your birth date and your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The next Thursday I'll be 85. I was born on June 29, 1910, in Odessa, then Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Odessa, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-D-E-S-S-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Could you describe your growing up in Odessa, your life in Odessa, in your early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Somehow, my memories go very far back. Do you-- do you imagine that I remember how my sister was born, and they moved my crib from one corner and put it in that place, her crib? My father came probably from a small town or Ukrainian shtetl Znamenka. And I think that he was a tailor. But he made good in Odessa. Besides the shop, we had something-- another-- together with the tailor shop, there was also a store, where he used to sell ready-to-make or ready-order suits. Because I remember where my mother or the nanny would take me there. So I remember the shop with counter and materials, and whatever or not. But in the back room-- some three or four sewing machines and bearded Jewish men in their typical caps, you know, chewing something. There was a woman. And sometimes, I saw some kind of a child. And obviously he made good because in my birth certificate, I remember, it was written that [NON-ENGLISH] Abraham, Abraham, son of Eliezer, son of so-so, Merchant of the First Guild. And the First Guild was something that the passport-- that who-- you could travel all over Russia, even to Moscow and St. Petersburg, which essentially was beyond the Jewish pale. And you could do that only-- you could go there only with special permits from the authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did your family consist of, Dr. Melezin?

speaker: interviewee
text: So before the war, in those years, my family consisted of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three children-- I, my sister Esther, my brother-- I think probably either [? Zachary ?] or [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and, of course, my father and my mother. But then my father was drafted into the Russian army when the war-- in 1914, the World War I. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember quite, you know, a lot some, I would say, fantasies of my mother. She had a painted portrait of herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the public schools in Odessa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Odessa, I didn't go to public schools because-- I don't know why. Because when my father was drafted in the army-- so around 1915, when I was about four and a half or five years old-- I was four and a half. It was winter. She liquidated the business-- I don't know on whose advice. And we moved out from Odessa. We moved out first to a city called Yelisavetgrad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It seems to me, in Russian-- in Soviet times, it used to be called Zinovyevsk. That's not important. And from there, we'll moved to Kherson. It is a larger city on the river Dnieper, a well-known port which used to export a, a, a lot of grain. It was very-- Dnieper, in that place, was a very large river, like Hudson, whose-- sea ships coming up to pick up wares, or whatever they had. And in Kherson, I know they-- my mother-- or whoever was there behind it-- bought a small hotel. I remember it was called [NON-ENGLISH], 'France.' And it was on the [NON-ENGLISH] Street, near the intersection with the Torgovaya Street. Torgovaya means the 'commerce' street-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], more or less. There I went-- I remember one thing that during our short sojourn, sojourn in Yelisavetgrad, I used to go to kindergarten. And now I remember the name of the proprietors of the kindergarten. It was a German or Dutch name-- Van der Flit. This is how far my memories go. Of course, there are some other family memories. My first very big beating I got because I took apart my sister's doll, who used to say, mama.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish community in the town that you had moved to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. As much-- as much as Odessa is concerned-- so in the living room, on one of the walls there was a portrait of Theodor Herzl, and under it, a small table with a Pushkin. And my mother used to take me sometimes and say, take a look. This is Jewish king.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I assume-- generally, I assume that we are-- we are a secularized family, but not in the same sense as here they say 'secular.' Because I don't remember going to synagogue in Odessa-- maybe, probably, we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know that my father used to come in a regular suit, you know, like a merchant-- well-to-do merchant, I would say. His golden, you know, chain was here, or whatever or not. And in Kherson, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used to get already-- about when I was eight or nine years old, I got a rebbe in Kherson. Beside that-- so let me start maybe telling you of Kherson, what has happened there. In Kherson, still-- in-- we came probably in Kherson probably about-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] end of 1915, I think, or '16, beginning '16. And there appeared another person in our family. I don't know. My mother fell in love-- in love with somebody else. And it started to be grudged. Now it was-- it started-- the family became split later because my, my mother wanted a divorce. And Beth din-- and each of my father and my mother wanted to have all children, all three of us. But Beth din has decided one goes with one, and two with the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father wanted me. And my mother wanted me, you know. And this created a very strong impression upon my siblings, you know. My brother-- sister-in-law, they never forgot this. They didn't know what was happening then. I, I knew. So, you know, it seems to me that I don't know what they did. But they drew lots. And it turned out that I am going with mother. And they two go with father. And this was-- And where did you go with your mother? To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my stepfather took us, in 1922, to Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he, he was from Vilna. Speaking frankly, I don't know what she found in him, except that he had a small voice and sung romantic, you know, Italian couplets, and whatever or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism during those early years in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The first--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: --anti-Semitic outbreak I experienced in Kherson, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the Ukraine declared independence. So the central government of Petliura could not control his subordinates-- [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the generals. And they were terribly anti-Semitic. There was a series of pogroms. So I remember that one day, they-- there was a rumor that there will be a pogrom in Kherson. And my nanny, who, whom I remember from very, very beginning-- probably she was given to my mother by her mother, you know-- took us to a monastery behind Vilna, you know. And we were there for two or three days. And then later, my mother came and to pick up. I remember one thing that my stepfather told-- that we organized a very good self-defense [NON-ENGLISH]-- Russian, I don't-- this what I remember. Then, of course, I had another experience-- anti-Semitic when I was-- so I was going to a [? press ?] school. But when they-- in '17, it seems to me, changed to a Ukrainian language. So my mother took me away. And put me in the preparatory class in a high school-- a commerce high school. And there, one day, was some kind of disturbances. We were outside the school. And some Ukrainian or Russian boys came up and pelted us with stones and, it seems to me, with something else. And right away, they pushed several of us and me. And I was bleeding here, you know, for a long time, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You continued your schooling in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Vilna-- so first of all, you know, I, I came to entirely different conditions. Still in Odessa and Kherson, I was, you know, living in urban area in a good apartment with electricity and even toilet, you know, which was quite an exceptional thing at that time. But when we came to Vilna, we went to suburbia, which was a row house with no toilet, no running water, and no electricity. And all the children around me was an entirely different class, you know. Here I spoke only Russian, a little bit-- a very little Ukrainian Yiddish. It means, not-- you know, the difference, let's say, the [NON-ENGLISH] Yiddish-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And everything looked to me very hard. Together with this whole environment of what the goal is. There is-- in the house, there was no well. We had to drag the well from, you know, across the street three houses up, or whatever it is. And of course, these Jewish children had a negative attitude towards me. Because here came a Russian aristocrat, they said to me. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time when you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at the time 11, 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And like now, I realize no, so that somehow that I rolled down-- you know, the feeling that I rolled down from a big, high peak to a deep ravine. And also, for the first time, I saw, you know, unpaved streets, you know, children running barefoot in whatever you may call clothes. But it was not clothes, you know? It was-- psychologically, it was just a big-- very unpleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go for your higher education--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: --as you got older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to a Russian high school. But in, in the next year, that Russian high school turned into Polish. By the way, I lost one year. Because when they gave me the exam from Polish-- so I wrote such tremendously stupid things that [LAUGHS] I don't believe how I could-- I-- [LAUGHS] when I look back. It is very hard to translate it into English. But I, I wrote idiotic dictate. So they put me back one year. I lost one year. It's why I graduated-- instead of 18-- '28, when I was-- you know, I graduated when I was 19. And the first three years was very, very hard for me on account of language. And then I never picked up a good Polish language. Because surrounding, I had that local eastern Polish language, which differs strongly from literal Polish, first of all, in pronunciation and some other things. But I became a kind of expert in this language and later even wrote some short stories about it after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after your high school years?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when I was in the fifth grade or so, my stepfather-- we didn't go along. First of all, I want to tell you that my father died in 1924. And the children remained with the greatmother. And greatmother died at the beginning-- or at the end of '24. They were left alone. And my mother took all possible steps to bring the children here. And they brought them illegally. And there was a lot of trouble on this account Because denunciation with their non-bribing bribery. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, when I was in the fifth grade or starting the sixth grade, my father-- who, by real profession, was a apothecary apprentice-- because his brother had a drug store in St. Petersburg. He was very educated man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, look. I had six grades. And you'll be now for you six grades. After that, you-- you can either work with me in that truck farm that we have-- vegetable truck farm that we have, or you do whatever you want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: But already at that time, I understood that if-- I had examples of my neighbor-- Jewish also truck farmer there, who-- and I saw what happened to his sons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So at that time, I understood. And my mother said to me, she said, let's try to, to study. She sold the rest of her jewelry. And I applied-- when I graduated sixth grade, I applied for a, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a loan. And I got from the board of the school a loan for certain-- because I was already on top of the school, you know, probably in one of the 50, all right. I graduated school without my stepfather paying a penny for it. And I went to university. As I was a Zionist, of Jabotinsky-- Jabotinsky Men. Therefore, my idea was to go to Israel. And I decided that I would be agronomist. And I started with agronomy at University of Stefan Batory Department of Agriculture. I went through the first year. On the second year, there was small incident between me and professor by the name Lelesz.

speaker: interviewer
text: He made the roll call. It came up-- Abraham Melezin, you know? Melezin is a neutral name. You don't know what's-- but Abraham, he said, we never had-- I, I never had a student who passed my class, you know. So here all my national pride jumped out. I stood up. I said, Professor, I won't permit you to flunk me. I left the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to study biology. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, accidentally, I had a free hour. Professor-- one of the professors was away. I went-- to kill an hour, I went to a lecture of geography. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What I would like to tell you now-- the whole life what I had is a kind of several links, sometimes not interconnected, sometimes touching each other, and some of them just interdigitated, you know. And this was a, a simple accident. I came. The professor very impressed me by way he presented his things, by his personality. Even-- I was so under his impression that when I started to teach, I took on many of his moves and tones in the progress of the class, and so on. But what was happened on this very thing, you know, very first lecture. He brought out a large atlas, German atlas, And about 10 minutes before the end of the class, he opened it somewhere-- [POINTS] around his finger, and put it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he said, this place called [NON-ENGLISH]. Where is it? In the class of [LAUGHS] geographers, you know, who are studying for geography, nobody knew. Here I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] accidentally was a spa, a Le Mans spa, south of Odessa where we used to go every single year from Odessa summertime. And I remember, you know, there every single in detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- so he looked at me, then goes the very same thing, you know, [LAUGHS] [POINTS] round with finger, puts it on the atlas. And what had he had to hit? He had to hit Znamenka in Ukraine, on the same page.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew also. Nobody knew. Then he went to other pages. And similarly, I knew everything-- whatever he did, [LAUGHS] whatever he asked. After-- then, after the end of the class, I saw that he spoke to his assistant, Dr. Rewenska, Wanda Rewenska.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she came up to me, asked me, what-- where do I study, what do I study, whether I would like to be-- transfer myself to geography. They would facilitate everything, and so on. So I said, what do geographers can do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Geography is my subject I love, you know. I, I read adventure stories, whatever or not. And I had an atlas, which my stepfather presented when I was bar mitzvahed, a Russian atlas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought-- so she told me if you want to do it, so you have to take this course, this course, this course, and that. I decided, let's take it. I wasn't sure whether I would take strict biology, or I will go to zoology and whatever else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how I started geography. But then I had to drop out because-- you know that 1929 crisis, which was so terrible in the United States was even more terrible in Poland. And I remember that our family simply, you know, almost starved. We didn't have money even to buy bread. So we used to buy it in a shop-- a small shop across the street. And a booklet-- she would write in-- [NON-ENGLISH], her name was-- she would write in date, what who took, what who took. And then we would pay pay off it when we could sell vegetables in summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you had to leave the university in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yes. I, I had to interrupt university because, you see, I had to work for my-- for paying, paying university. It was-- about, you know, in a year-- in a semester-- $278 a semester, or a trimester, I don't remember, you know. And I used to tutor. And-- but when the, the crisis started, there was no tutoring.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I volunteered to Polish Army. But I didn't-- you know, as a student of-- university student, I had the choice of arms, so to speak. So I asked them to put in a topographic school. And then I-- a topographic school, it was-- out of reach for Jews-- no Jews there, and only selected.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, I asked my professor one. I asked my professor, who was the second one-- Professor [NON-ENGLISH], Professor [NON-ENGLISH]. We [? sink ?] arms and whatever or not, I went into topographic school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Essentially was an officer-- I was a reserve officer in Polish Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I returned back-- When was that, Mr. Melezin?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the beginning of 1935. I get up-- I was 18 months in school. I returned to university. First of all, I had a little bit money from from that military school, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism while you were in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was terrible. It was just terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I remember like now, you know, one day, a sergeant came up. Not a sergeant, because he was-- he was a lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came up to me in the morning Appell. And, you know, he took his hand [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and drew my cheek. And I was lightly shaven. So he hit me, you know. You kike! Don't you know that you have to come shaved, whatever it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. But what I can tell you? I was a good--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're just going to change the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Change the tape now.
